text: The patient is positioned in lithotomy, and an oriole proctoscope is introduced at a slight angle to access the rectum affected by radiation proctitis. A four percent formalin solution is instilled into the scope, which is kept at an angle to prevent spillage onto the perianal skin. The scope is rotated slowly to bathe the rectal mucosa, causing the mucosa to blanch. The procedure is performed under theatre lighting without additional power sources.

After ten minutes, the surgeon aspirates the remaining formalin from the aureal proctoscope. The previously erythematous rectal mucosa now appears blanched, indicating the effect of the formalin treatment.

The rectal mucosa appears blanched after the formalin treatment, indicating reduced bleeding. The aureal proctoscope is used to manage bleeding in patients with anticoagulation drugs, such as clopidogrel and warfarin, and those with radiation-affected piles.

The surgeon introduces the scope to visualize the second-degree piles, using a swab to locate them at positions three, seven, eleven, nine, and twelve o'clock. Sutures are placed to pin the mucosa, with six to eight sutures in total. The surgeon completes a surgical knot at six o'clock, cutting the end of the ventral suture, demonstrating wrist rotation to show the tip.
The needle is carefully passed through the affected mucosa, maneuvered at an angle to prevent tearing, and then withdrawn.
